segments.text: Between New Zealand and India.
We are hearing first of all from the New Zealand Council of Trade
Unions,
Rachel McIntosh.
Excellent. We'll hand over to you.
We have 10 minutes set down for your hearing today.
I'm sure you've done this before. You're welcome to leave time for
questions within that. Thank you.
Kia ora, Tim. Tena tatou ko Rachel McIntosh toku
ingoa. I'm the vice president of the New Zealand Council of Trade Unions, Te
Kawai Kaimahi. We represent the interests of our 32
affiliated trade unions and more than 370,000
working people across the country.
Appreciate the opportunity to submit on the treaty
examination of the New Zealand India Free Trade Agreement.
So the CTU approach on trade agreements is to identify possible
risks to the New Zealand economy, to employment and workers' rights, both
here and overseas, and to local businesses, while also recognizing the
advantages some sectors may gain from better access to foreign markets.
So we're living in a turbulent time, and New
Zealand has a reasonably concentrated trade portfolio with
relatively few trading partners.
So it is sensible to diversify our trade
relationships where possible, and trade agreements can be a useful
mechanism for achieving this.
But trade agreements must be well-designed, and the New Zealand public must have
adequate time and information to possibly consult on the development
and ratification of any agreement.
We have major concerns about this free trade agreement
on both fronts. So the two central concerns we'll speak to
today are the inadequate labor provisions in the agreement
and the unacceptably short timeframe for treaty examination.
So for these reasons, we cannot support this deal.
And I'm now going to hand over to Jack Foster, my colleague, who will make some
further detailed comments on these issues. Jack.
Kia ora. Thank you, Rachel, and thank you,
Chair. Apologies, I had slight technical difficulties.
So yeah, I'll just
expand on those two points as Rachel mentioned.
So first of all, labor rights are unavailable to many workers in India, and
there are an estimated 11 million people living in conditions of modern
slavery in that country. So that includes forced labor and forced
commercial sexual exploitation.
So given these major labor rights problems in
India, the CTU would expect a trade agreement with that country to
contain a strong and enforceable labor chapter that would commit India
to materially improving its labor standards and protections.
This is necessary to protect New Zealand against the risk that it will import
goods made in India in exploitative conditions, and it's also necessary to
protect Kiwi companies from being put at an unfair commercial
disadvantage due to the lower labor costs that businesses located
in India can rely on.
So unfortunately, the trade and sustainable development chapter in this agreement
uses largely non-binding language and is not covered by the
dispute settlement chapter anyway, which makes it unenforceable.
So it will do little, if anything, to support labor standards and
protections.
So the CTU cannot support an agreement with such a weak labor chapter, and in our
written submission, we set out areas that we expect a modern trade
agreement should cover with respect to labor rights and protections.
So our second major concern is the inadequate timeframe for the treaty
examination process. So the secrecy of trade negotiations
has been a longstanding problem in New Zealand that the CTU's often commented on.
The standard practice is that the text of an FTA is generally not made
available for public comment until after it has been signed.
And this makes genuine public consultation quite difficult, as by the time an
agreement is signed, it already has lots of political momentum.
This process was replicated in the negotiation of the NZ India FTA,
but the negotiations were even more secretive than usual, with the CTU and
other civil society organizations not even allowed to view and
comment on draft text in confidence, which we've done in the
past with multiple trade agreements.
We understand the secrecy was at the request of the Indian government.
Nevertheless, given that the whole process has been behind closed doors
in which to review and make submissions to the select committee on this
agreement.
This timeframe seems to be driven by electoral calculations rather than good
governance. So the length of the consultation material alone justifies a
far longer consultation period. If we exclude tariff lines, the text
of the agreement runs to 569 pages.
Then there's an additional seven side letters, and then the national
interest
for the public in three weeks.
But more important than the level of material to examine, this is an agreement that
will sign New Zealand up to binding obligations
for decades. Reasonable people will disagree over whether some of these
obligations are in the national interest, and this needs to be openly debated.
But this
remarks there, but in our written submission, we also outlined concerns we
have regarding the commitment that New Zealand will promote foreign direct
investment with the aim of increasing such investment to $20
billion U.S.,
and also concerns regarding the productivity action plans that have been
agreed for numerous horticulture exports.
Thanks for the opportunity to speak about this agreement, and we reiterate that we
cannot support the agreement due to the weak labor provisions it
contains, and we also request that the consultation period on this treaty is
significantly extended to enable proper public debate about its
pros and cons. Thank you.
All right. Thank you for taking time to share your views this morning.
First question from Tim Costley
Yeah, thank you, Mr. Chair. So
you've both commented on the timeframe.
I assume you're both aware that that's sort of set down in law that every
treaty gets the exact same period of time, the EU
free trade agreement.
And the second thing, I'll just give it to you now so you can comment on both.
So, I mean, the timeframe isn't in question.
What's your alternative, that we should just not approve the agreement?
Obviously, you're speaking in opposition to it.
Is that going to help your members if we miss out on at least $1 billion
extra coming into our economy, creating more jobs, creating better-paying jobs?
You'd rather just not see that at all. Is that what you're saying?
You think nothing is a better
outcome?
Thank you. Sorry,
you cut out briefly in the middle of your second question, but to the first
question, I would say that the timeframe is just inadequate, so that needs to be
addressed.
Yeah, but that's not been addressed.
So to this agreement-
It's been set down in law.
Yeah, I think it's actually your internet connection.
Most of what you said was cutting out. Rachel's been really good.
So the timeframe isn't something that this committee can control.
Um-
But what's your alternative, that we just don't bother trading with
India? That we don't try and grow the money coming into this
job to create better-paying jobs
and more jobs for the kind of people that your union is meant to represent?
Yeah, sorry, my internet connection has been
faulty. Yeah, so we would say that, first of all, the economic
benefits of the agreement are very modest, so it's important to bear that in mind.
Yeah, they said that 20 years ago about China, and clearly it's not true.
Yeah, well, time will tell. But we also have the
economic
benefits of the agreement are also contingent on New Zealand making good on the FDI
commitments, which
seem very fanciful in our view.
There's no automatic link between reducing tariff lines and
better jobs becoming available. It depends on
what industries get grown. It depends on
what domestic decisions New Zealand makes to support the growth of good
jobs. So the kind of idea that you just reduce
trade barriers and that is
good for everyone is not supported by the statistics.
It tends to benefit,
flows down to benefits for wider New Zealand workers is a totally different
question, and we haven't seen that necessarily happen with
trade agreements in the past.
Okay, look, we're over time, so we'll have to jump to our next submitter now, but
thank you very much for your time this morning.
Next up, we have Chris Newman.
Right. We see you've joined now,
Mr. Newman.
Up my video.
There we go. Excellent. Thank you very much.
Have I got-
We have five minutes set down for you this morning
... control there?
We'll hand straight over to you. Thank you.
Yep. Okay. Chris Newman. I have professional
qualifications in education. I have a degree in sociology.
I've lived overseas 25 years of my life.
I've been married to an Asian woman for 27 years and have two
East-West children,
so I'm qualified to speak from the front lines of living.
I've also lived in India. We have many concerns on the
social level. We can argue economics all day,
but the problem we're having in the city here
is
people are coming from foreign countries and replicating all the
downside of their culture and their traditions and their behavior,
which is not compatible with or friendly to
our way, and we are the host community.
MPs are meant to protect us. Their first
job for an MP is to protect the nation.
We are not being protected against people who come here with values that are
hostile and inimical to us, many of whom have
submitted fraudulent documents to get into the country,
and the laws are not being applied to them.
We have the example, for example, of 460 Sikh truck
drivers who produced forged documents to get into
New Zealand, and they were never returned home even though they violated our
Crimes Act.
All this business was sold to the New Zealand public as if we were going to be
bringing in high-skilled individuals like rocket scientists and
brain surgeons, and instead we've got people running pizza parlors,
Uber drivers,
checkout ladies. All the local people are being pushed out of
employment. My son, who's a university student, can no longer
find easy access to
part-time employment during the holidays because all that work's been soaked up by
cheap immigrant labor. The Indian people who are
brought here are also being exploited by their fellows, as
they are exploited in India, which is what I saw boldly when I
lived there. And there is no doubt that there are now sufficient
number of Indians in New Zealand, 350,000 plus,
5.8% of our population, to run their own rackets
and systems in a parallel society to ours, and
the MPs are going to tell us-... how much money we're going to make.
No one's done any work on the black economy, how much tax is not
being paid. What's the social cost to us from having
people turning up in hospital who've been intergenerational cousin
marriages for centuries, and their birth rates are
producing children with birth defects, and we are
expected to carry all the downsides socially of this.
What happens when there is an economic downturn and all these
immigrants who are not rocket scientists or brain
surgeons, who are simple people, end up on the unemployment roll,
burdening our social welfare system?
Also, our infrastructure is stretched to breaking point in
Auckland. There's huge traffic lines of people trying to drive
into the city. Bringing mass migration has flooded and
overloaded the infrastructure that I paid for, my parents,
my grandparents paid for, for us, and the immigrants
are getting full benefit of all of our systems without ever
contributing to the building up of what we've got, let
alone about the having to rebuild them to
cope with the mass overload.
So we are facing also a demographic genocide over the
long term with the open door through these agreements.
We know they're never honored in terms of protecting us.
It's the new group coming in. The laws don't apply to them,
only to us. So they break our DEI laws.
You can go around Indian businesses, and you'll see only Indian
employees. What on earth are they doing here?
Why have we got Indian immigrant real estate agents?
This long list of people coming here from this
particular deal in this country is just an insult
to us. We were never asked if
we wanted any of this stuff before it was even proposed, and
it's being presented to us like a done deal.
We don't want it. The New Zealand people don't want this invasion.
We don't want uncontrollable colonization from,
for example, Islam has many, many points which violate
our active classification.
Right, Mr. Noon, we've come to the end of the time set down for you this morning,
but appreciate your insights.
And this is being put through with no challenge at all. Okay, I'm finished.
All right.
Thank you.
Okay, we come now to Jim Capo.
Good morning, Mr. Capo.
I see you've joined us there. You might like to unmute your
microphone and turn on your video, and then we'll be away. Excellent.
Oh, yep. Sorry about that.
Good to see you. Thank you.
I'm sure you're aware how the process works. We have five minutes set down.
I'll hand straight to you.
Yes, thank you very much. Appreciate being given the opportunity to speak this
morning. In my
submission, I did mention that I'm an immigrant to New Zealand.
You can tell from my
accent. I've been here 16 years and proud citizen.
I like to present my New Zealand passport wherever I travel around the world.
I wanted to submit this,
basically speak against the
acceptance of the current
free trade agreement, which I note in the presentation, this is
actually called an examination of the...
It's an international treaty examination.
So I feel like this should be marketed as a treaty, not
a straight trade agreement. There's a lot in this that's really more around a
treaty. I do agree with a lot of what was just said by the
NZCTU.
They were asked the question about the timeline being
restricted by law, and I think the correct answer would be then
to reject the current agreement that was negotiated in secret,
have everybody a chance to review it, revise the sections
that are in debate or discussion, and resubmit.
That would be the correct process that I would submit.
One of the reasons I wanted to present was because when I was in the United States,
I was involved in several grassroots efforts to block some of
what were called free trade agreements in the United States.
One of those was the Central American Free Trade Agreement.
That was a similar situation where the government was trying to say, "Look,
we're doing this under fast track. There's rules.
We have to vote on it within a certain amount of time." There was
grassroots resistance they didn't expect. The time was running out.
The government was starting to worry.
They brought in the ambassadors of the five Central American nations to
do a roadshow around the country. And, I was at the
first
presentation they gave.
And they weren't very well prepared.
They allowed the
audience to ask whatever questions they wanted, and they got questions they didn't
really want to answer. And
it didn't work out for them in the end.
But afterwards, there was a nice little meet and greet and
over a couple of drinks and
snacks, we were able to talk to the ambassadors, and I had a direct
conversation with the Ambassador of Costa Rica,
to the
United States of Costa Rica. And he said that the reason Costa
Rica wanted to sign this deal with the United States is they had a similar
negotiation going on with the European Union, but the European
Union wanted the Central American countries to
give up their sovereignty before they had an economic
agreement. And the United States was offering some economic
benefits, direct benefits, in exchange for the
ceding of sovereignty of the Central American nations into this
global framework of a treaty. So I guess my
question to thePanel and the people that are going to be voting on this
would be what guarant-- This is a treaty.
New Zealand is giving up certain authority to negotiate, uh,
in the future. So, what concrete
benefits are we getting in giving up this authority?
And if those benefits aren't concrete and well understood by the
public, this treaty should be rejected as is,
renegotiated, and resubmitted. That's basically my position.
All right. Thank you very much for your time.
We've got time for a quick question for Mr. Capo, if anyone has one.
Otherwise, we'll move to our next submitter.
All right. Thank you very much for speaking to your submission this morning.
Appreciate it.
Appreciate it.
Okay, next up, we have Gary Judd AC.
Excellent. All right.
Looks like he's just joined us.
Good morning, Mr. Judd. Can you hear us?
Just need to bring up your camera and turn off your
mute.
Ah, perfect. There we go. All right.
Good morning. Welcome along to the committee this morning.
We've received your submission. We have five minutes set down, and we'll hand
straight over to you. Thank you.
Unfortunately, we can't hear you at the moment.
Okay, he appears to have dropped out.
Let me just see if we can get him back in.
Okay,
we'll give Mr. Judd a moment to come back in if
he can.
Okay.
He doesn't appear to be.
All right, we'll move straight to William Fitzgerald then, our next submitter.
Thank you.
Okay. Can you all see and hear me?
Yes, we can. Thank you.
All right. Slight hiccup with the previous submitter there, but, uh, we'll jump
straight to you, Mr. Fitzgerald. We have five minutes set down, and I'll let you
crack into it. Thanks.
Thank you for the opportunity to speak to the committee.
Because speaking time is limited, I will focus today on the migration and labor
market provisions of the India/New Zealand Free Trade Agreement.
I speak as a New Zealand citizen and domestic mathematics major approaching
graduation, so I have a direct interest in technical employment in New
Zealand.
The FTA includes temporary employment entry, working holiday and student
placements, along with post-study work-related pathways.
In fact, it's described the equivalent of 1,667
three-year temporary employment entry visas per year, capped at
5,000 at any one time, as well as up to 1,000 working holiday
places per year.
Immigration into New Zealand is already high relative to the size of our existing
population. Stats NZ recorded a net gain of
54,200 non-New Zealand citizens in the December
2025 year. Against a population of about
5.34 million, that is equivalent to adding the population of a small
city to our country in a single year.
The FTA may not directly create residence or family reunification rights,
but if it does not explicitly prevent later transitions into other visa
categories, then temporary entry, post-study work, and
employment access may still interact with employer sponsorship,
residence pathways, and family reunification settings, creating
a risk of increasing long-term immigration from India overall.
New Zealand's labor market is already weak.
In particular, while there are shortages of senior software engineers,
cybersecurity specialists, or experienced systems architects,
there is no domestic shortage of entry-level IT workers or recent technical
graduates. Yet the FTA expands post-study and employment
pathways for Indian graduates of New Zealand institutions.
This reduces opportunities for citizen graduates who are trying to begin our
careers, and it does not address the technology sector's actual skills
shortages. There is also a sovereignty problem.
If these were ordinary visa settings, a future government could abolish them
through normal domestic processes.
But once migration access is placed inside a bilateral trade agreement,
any later restriction becomes diplomatically and legally more complicated.
My position is not that New Zealand should not trade with India.
It is that trade policy should not be used as a vehicle for migration policy.
If Parliament wishes to expand migration pathways, it should do so through domestic
law and seek explicit democratic consent.
I therefore oppose ratification of the India/New Zealand FTA in its present
form. Thank you.
Excellent. Thank you for your submission this morning.
Have we got questions for Mr.
Fitzgerald?
All right. Thank you very much for coming on this morning, William.
Appreciate your time.
Okay. Thank you.
Okay. We'll jump back to, um, Mr. Judd.
I believe he is back online.
Hopefully, we're able to hear from him this time.
Hello?
Yes. Good morning. We can hear you this time.
Yes. Good.
Okay, thank you.
I am a great supporter of free trade
agreementsHowever, problem with this
one is
its affirmation of the United Nations
Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous People, UNDRIP.
It also affirms New Zealand's position on
UNDRIP. In legal language,
affirmation signals acceptance of a commitment.
Earlier free trade agreements did not do that.
They simply said that
UNDRIP and New Zealand's
position on UNDRIP was noted.
It isn't
domestic law unless Parliament incorporates it,
but courts can still use treaties when
interpreting legislation and in other
decision-making. Now that matters here.
Justice Susan Glazebrook has written that UNDRIP is becoming
increasingly embedded in New Zealand's legal
framework.
Despite that, affirmation in
this FTA still matters. It strengthens
and legitimizes that movement.
In July 2023, New Zealand's
representatives at the UN
told that body that New Zealand is committed to
upholding UNDRIP, and that self-determination
requires indigenous peoples to participate
effectively in decision-making that affects
them. As far as I can see, that is the latest
statement of New Zealand's position.
Since government decisions affect all New Zealanders,
the implication is broad.
In substance, it points towards co-governance,
tribal vetoes, and even tribal rule.
That should be contrasted with New Zealand's earlier position.
In 2007, Helen Clark's government told the United
Nations it could not endorse UNDRIP,
because endorsement would imply special rights,
a veto over democratic decision-making, and different
classes of citizenship.
It said that those outcomes
were incompatible with New Zealand's democratic and constitutional
arrangements.
Te Heuheu
chose those concerns were not fanciful.
Present government did not formally change New Zealand's
position, it simply stopped work on
implementation.
Even that direction has not been obeyed,
because commitment to UNDRIP has been put into the Free
Trade Agreement.
Now, at 9:30, I'm presenting on
behalf
Hobbs Pledge, and I'll then explain how this
problem can be resolved. In the meantime, I'm happy to answer any questions.
Excellent. Do we have any questions for Mr. Judd at this point?
All right. Well, I look forward to hearing from you then again, later
on with the joint submission. Thank you very much for your time.
Thank you.
Okay, we move now to Ryan Henderson.
Okay. Is Mr. Henderson
with us?
Okay, just joining in now.
Morning, Mr. Henderson.
Good morning.
Tena koutou, chair and members. My name is Ryan Henderson.
I am an undergraduate economics student at the University of Auckland,
and I have read the full
National Interest Analysis and the commission modeling by the government,
and read large parts of the 1,300-page
text
of this agreement.
Everything I say this morning sits in those documents.
I'm asking the committee to recommend that the House
decline to bring this into agreement
on its current terms and for a minority report to be written.
So starting with what we get. The modeling puts the gain at about
$401 million a year by 2037.
That is 700th of 1% of the economy,
which is less than the government borrows in a fortnight.
Three-quarters of the new exports to India are not new at
all. They are sales redirected from customers we already sell to.
That's diversification dressed up as growth.
And two of our three largest export earners go
backwards. On the same modeling, dairy and services both end up
smaller by 2036 than they would be without the deal.
The money migrants send home was also not counted.
On Australia's numbers, about 32,000 new
arrivals sending money to family
would wipe out the whole
gain.The wins we
are sold also come heavily capped.
Wine under $5 a bottle stays at
150% forever. That is most of our bulk export
Sauvignon Blanc.
Our premium wine still pays 25%
after 10 years of phasing.
On Manuka honey, the headline cut reaches only
the dearest product, a few percent of what we send
abroad. The rest stays at 66%.
Apples and kiwifruit get small seasonal quotas with tariffs that never go
away, and India can suspend those if it judges we have
fallen short of our obligations.
We have done better before. China took our wine to zero by
2012 and opened up for beef. The EU deal,
led by a member of this committee,
did far more on wine, on enforcement, and on climate.
The same trading partner gave us a stronger deal.
Here, beef and dairy are shut out completely.
The heaviest costs fall on workers.
Youth unemployment is 14.4% and climbing.
This deal opens an uncapped channel for Indian students and an
uncapped specialist channel work visa for Indian firms
straight into that labor market, and it strips our power to cap
either of them later. The wage protection meant to cover these workers
doesn't hold, and the government has said so.
The national interest analysis admits in writing that there are,
and I quote, "practical challenges in ensuring compliance with such
obligations, as these workers will be paid by their
home employer in India."
The union saw it coming. E tū says the people coming
in are ripe for exploitation. The maritime
union says it will tell labor in no uncertain terms how
disappointed it is,
and India has not signed the two core conventions on the right to
organize and to bargain. The chapter meant to protect workers
is the one chapter India can never be taken to a panel over.
And then there is the investment. We've promised to push 33
billion
New Zealand dollars into India over 15 years
while we are short of houses and hospitals here.
India promises nothing back, and India alone
decides whether we have delivered.
If it rules we have not, it can claw back access our
exporters have won with no independent umpire.
When our wine growers were asked at this committee how India could pull those
tariffs back, it had no figure for what we are meant to invest.
Right. Thank you, Mr. Henderson. We've come to the end of your time this morning.
We appreciate your insights.
Well done-
Thank you
... on all that reading.
Thank you.
Okay, we come now to Richard Arlidge.
Good morning, Mr. Arlidge.
Just need you to come up on screen. Can see you there,
but not your
video, and you're on mute.
Right. Can you see me-
Right, yes
... and hear me?
We can hear you, but-- and, yes, now we can see you. Okay, over to you.
Thank you.
All right. My name is Richard Arlidge, and thank you for this opportunity to
present to you. Given my time is brief, I'll be terse, and it's
not without some considerable annoyance that I have to spend time pointing out the
obvious and what I, as a taxpayer, pay and mandate others to
do on my behalf. It is to look out for our collective best
interest as a nation of New Zealand.
By way of my background, I was born in New Zealand, and my ancestors on
both sides of my family coincidentally arrived in this country by ship in
1856. I consider myself a law-abiding indigenous
New Zealander, and I've spent my entire working career in matters relating to
real property, its assessment for acquisition and disposal,
mortgage authoriz-authorization, insurance, and more especially
in various forms of its leasehold occupation.
I state here and now that I'm not an expert in free trade agreements.
That said, I've spent my working lifetime perusing legal documents and in their
relationship to property, and so I have more than a modicum of experience with the
law and its documentation than many of my compatriots.
And with my hand firmly on my heart, I can attest to the truth of the saying that
the devil is in the detail.
You've just heard from Gary Judd KC, who having spent a distinguished
lifetime in matters legal by nature in and around courts of law,
he is acutely qualified and aware of how legal matters
might be interpreted by our judiciary, especially one that
appears these days to be rather more comfortable to push the boundaries.
Given his findings, and also those views expressed by Dame Professor Elizabeth
Rata, which I proffer would hit a chord with the majority of most
reasonably well-informed New Zealanders experiences, our country is
now very much at a crossroads where our democracy and the very
sovereignty of our Parliament stands to be dramatically challenged and
changed. And it's all because of a reputedly benign
affirmation on something that there has never been a clear, unequivocal
mandate from this country's citizens.
Surely, the principal purpose of us entering the FTA with India is to provide
economic benefits to New Zealand.
Of and in itself, the United Nations Declaration of Independence,
UNDRIP or UNDRIPAs you like to pronounce it, has nothing
whatsoever to do with achieving economic benefits for New
Zealand or India. So given what I have just covered and
my own reservations expressed as to its relevance in my written submission,
the questions I respectfully would suggest you
seek to have answered are, one, what is the rationale and
purpose of inserting a requirement for New Zealand to affirm UNDROP
when we know that in 2007, our own permanent resident
to the United Nations, Rosemary Banks, and
with the endorsement of our Prime Minister at the time, Helen Clark, spelt out in
some significant detail why New Zealand would not sign up to
it, and why those reasons have never been addressed,
let alone overturned, not least of all by the will
and want of the majority of this country's citizens.
Two, we know that our affirmation of UNDROP will almost certainly
rekindle seditious he puapua-like aspirations that
go to the heart of our current democracy and sovereignty of our Parliament,
and that this will involve very substantial costs, both financially,
socially and constitutionally, should this proceed.
So where is the cost-benefit analysis to justify this UNDROP
affirmation objective, and where again is the mandate?
I would suggest to you the answers are obvious, and that this has nothing to do
with economic trade and benefit, and everything to do with an ideological
change to our country's constitutional operation.
I beg you to consider seeking answers to these questions, and unless
very clear and concise reasons can be given that
outweigh the now obvious risks and likely very substantial
costs, then I earnestly ask you to recommend the removal of the UNDROP
reference before there is any formal ratification of the FTA.
Thank you.
All right. Thank you very much for your time this morning.
Appreciate your insights. Cheers.
Okay, we come next to Helma Vermeulen.
Hello.
Good morning. Excellent.
Good morning to you.
We can't see you.
Okay.
You see me now?
Yes, we can. Excellent. Okay. We've five minutes for you this morning. Over to you.
This trade agreement, it's a highly conditional economic
integration agreement. It is downright catastrophic for New
Zealand. Like it or not, our lives and our
will be shaped by this trade deal. To me, the result of this trade
deal gives me pervasive feeling of
but rather a chaotic future for New Zealand.
The question that arises is that the moment India
back against allowing GMO products, and according to the PM
of India, the subject is very sensitive.
This trade agreement significantly includes agriculture and
horticulture product, honey, wine, kiwi fruits, and apples.
According to the Farmers Weekly, India has the right to suspend
these tariffs if certain conditions are not met.
This condition could be that imported produce was in contact with
genetically modified crops in New Zealand.
I do not understand why some political parties will push
GMO and the HSNO amendment
bill and likewise push the trade agreement with India.
This does not make sense. Why would you want to introduce GMO
crops while at the drop of a hat, India can stop
importing our product for this reason?
On the 16th of September 2025, in the first post,
wrote an article that India prohibited the import of USA

and strong local communities. When you rapidly
alter the demographic landscape of a nation without breathing room for natural
integration, community trust breaks down.
Neighbors stop knowing neighbors.
A fractured low-trust society is incredibly
easy for a centralized bureaucracy to manage.
When a population is divided horizontally, fighting amongst themselves over
straining infrastructure, healthcare, and housing, they lack the unified strength
to look vertically and hold the political and corporate elite
accountable.We've seen this pattern across the Western world.
Governments mismanage economic and border policies until a predictable
crisis occurs, and then the state steps in with the very solution it wanted to
implement all along. They will use this chaos to justify a
hyper-control grid. Digital identity tracking, increased
financial surveillance, and aggressive enforcement of internet regulations
under the guise of managing public safety and social harmony. But Mr.
Chairman, the true apex of this control grid is the push towards a programmable
currency. Proponents will argue that the digital trade and financial
chapters of this agreement are merely about economic efficiency.
But in reality, and make no mistake, agreements that lock us into global
digital standards lay the precise legal and technological
foundations for a central bank digital currency.
This is not about the convenience of digital payments.
This is about transforming money from a medium of economic
exchange to a conditional permission-based system.
When the state controls a programmable currency, they gain
unprecedented power to orchestrate serious institutional abuse.
They can program your money with an expiry date for spending.
They can geo-fence your digital wallet, so it only functions within a small radius
of your home during a mandate. They can instantly block your ability to buy fuel,
meat, or even travel if you exceed an arbitrary quota.
And they can freeze your access to your own hard-earned wealth with a single line
of code, bypassing due process and the courts entirely.
By tying New Zealand into a hyper-centralized global framework, we're leaving the
door wide open for financial weaponization against our own citizens.
We must protect the absolute anonymity of physical
cash, and we must reject any agreement which inches
us closer to a system where survival requires political
permission. Mr. Chairman, this FTA binds New Zealand tighter
to a global system that demands we surrender our independent choices.
It treats our citizens like interchangeable economic units rather than humans
with a unique heritage, culture, and homeland. There is an alternative.
True resilience does not come from signing away our laws and borders to an
international consensus. True security lies in decoupling from this
hyper-centralized grid. It lies in fostering local
self-reliance, protecting our domestic workforce, and maintaining a high-trust,
decentralized society where the state is forced to leave its citizens alone.
Please stand up for a future where New Zealand remains a sovereign home, not an
outsourced, tracked, and financialized economic zone.
All right.
